# Named Entity Recognition for Code-Mixed Indian Archaeological Texts: A Low-Resource Approach using mBERT, CRF, and Focal Loss

## 1. Abstract
Extracting specific entities from archaeological texts is pretty tough, especially when you don't have a lot of data. In the Indian context, it gets even harder because the text is heavily code-mixed—people write Hindi archaeological terms using English script. In this project, we tackle this problem by building a Named Entity Recognition (NER) system to pull out Artifacts, Locations, and Materials from these texts. 

Since we didn't have much data, we started with just 126 annotated sentences and used data augmentation to push it to around 1,200 sentences. Our model uses Multilingual BERT (mBERT) combined with a Conditional Random Field (CRF) layer to make sure the sequence tags make sense. We also noticed that some classes were super rare, so we used Focal Loss to help the model pay attention to them. Our final setup got a weighted F1 score of 0.7724, which is much better than basic baselines like IndicBERT or direct transfers of models like ArcheoBERTje. Overall, this shows a practical way to do NER for a very niche, low-resource domain without needing millions of words for pre-training.

## 2. Introduction
These days, there is a big push to digitize historical and archaeological records. But just scanning documents isn't enough; we need to actually extract information from them to build things like knowledge graphs. Named Entity Recognition (NER) is usually the first step for this. In archaeology, we want to extract specific things like excavation sites, ancient materials, and artifacts.

But doing this for Indian archaeological texts is really challenging. First off, reports from places like the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) use a lot of Hindi-English code-mixing. You'll see standard English grammar mixed with Romanized Hindi terms like *Stupa* or *Pradakshina Patha*. Another huge issue is that there is almost no annotated data available for this. Also, the words can be very confusing. For example, "terracotta" could be a material or the artifact itself depending on how it's used in the sentence.

Some researchers have solved similar problems before. A great example is *ArcheoBERTje*, where they trained a Dutch model on millions of archaeological words. But we just don't have that kind of data for Indian archaeology. We actually tried their approach at first, but it didn't work well because of the code-mixing and our tiny dataset. We also tried IndicBERT since it's meant for Indian languages, but it struggled with the English technical jargon.

So, we decided to try a different approach. We used mBERT since it handles multiple languages decently, added a CRF layer to fix weird tag sequences, and used Focal Loss to deal with the fact that some entities almost never show up. 

**Our main contributions are:**
* Creating what we believe is the first code-mixed Indian archaeological NER dataset.
* Showing that combining mBERT with gazetteer embeddings and CRF works well when you don't have enough data for full domain pre-training.
* Using Focal Loss in an NER setup to fix extreme class imbalance.
* Providing a clear analysis of why code-mixing messes up standard tokenizers.

## 3. Related Work
In the past, NER systems mostly used rule-based methods or statistical models like Hidden Markov Models and CRF. These were okay, but they needed a lot of manual feature engineering and usually broke down when they saw new words. Later on, models like BiLSTM-CRF became popular because they learned features automatically.

Nowadays, Transformer models like BERT are the standard. For specific fields, researchers usually pre-train a BERT model on a huge amount of domain text, creating models like BioBERT (for medical text) or SciBERT. As mentioned earlier, Brands et al. did this for Dutch archaeology with *ArcheoBERTje*. 

But our situation is a bit different. We are dealing with a zero-shot, low-resource scenario. We don't have millions of words to pre-train on. Also, dealing with Hindi-English code-mixing adds another layer of difficulty. While models like IndicBERT exist for Indian languages, they aren't great at handling the heavy English technical vocabulary used in archaeology. That's why we focused on tweaking the architecture itself (using CRF and Focal Loss) rather than just throwing more data at it.

## 4. Dataset
Since there was no existing dataset, we had to build one ourselves. We collected sentences from ASI excavation reports and some NCERT history books. 

We used the standard BIO (Begin, Inside, Outside) format for tagging. We decided on five main entity types:
* **ART (Artifact):** Things made by humans (e.g., *pottery shards*, *steatite seals*).
* **LOC (Location):** Places and excavation sites (e.g., *Harappa*, *Lothal*).
* **PER (Person):** Historical figures or researchers.
* **MAT (Material):** Raw materials (e.g., *terracotta*, *bronze*).
* **CON (Construction):** Architectural structures (e.g., *stupa*, *citadel*).

To make sure our tags were right, two of us annotated a subset of the data independently. We got a Cohen’s Kappa score of 0.82, which means we mostly agreed. However, annotation was sometimes really confusing. The MAT vs ART distinction was especially tricky. Deciding whether "bronze" was a material modifying an object or the object itself caused most of our disagreements.

One more thing we noticed was extreme class imbalance. About 85% of our tokens were just "O" (Outside) tags. Things like LOC and ART showed up fairly often, but CON and MAT were super rare. Because of this, we couldn't just use our original 126 sentences. We had to use data augmentation (like swapping entities using a gazetteer list and replacing synonyms) to artificially grow the dataset to around 1,200 sentences. 

## 5. Methodology
We didn't want to overcomplicate things, so we built our pipeline using components that specifically target our problems.

### 5.1 Model Architecture
We chose `bert-base-multilingual-cased` (mBERT) as our base model. We actually picked mBERT over IndicBERT because mBERT seemed to handle the mix of English and Romanized Hindi subwords a bit better. 
To give the model a hint, we also used a gazetteer (a list of known archaeological terms). If a word was in our list, we created a small embedding for it and attached it to mBERT's output. 

We then passed this into a BiLSTM layer just to help capture the sequence context better, and finally into a Conditional Random Field (CRF) layer. The CRF is really important here because it prevents the model from making silly mistakes, like predicting an `I-ART` tag right after an `O` tag without a `B-ART` first.

### 5.2 Tokenization Issues
One big headache was tokenization. Because the Hindi words are written in English script (like *Pradakshina*), mBERT’s tokenizer chops them up into tiny, meaningless subwords. To fix this, we only passed the first piece of the chopped-up word to the loss function and told the model to just ignore the rest.

### 5.3 Focal Loss
Normally, NER models use Cross-Entropy loss. But because 85% of our words were just "O" tags, the model would get lazy and just predict "O" for everything, ignoring our rare classes completely. 
To fix this, we swapped to Focal Loss. Simply put, Focal Loss looks at how confident the model is. If the model is easily predicting the "O" tags, Focal Loss tells it to stop worrying about them and forces it to focus its learning on the hard, rare classes like MAT and CON.

## 6. Experiments
We ran our code on a standard GPU setup using PyTorch and Hugging Face. We tested a few different baselines to see if our approach actually made a difference.

We compared against:
* **BiLSTM-CRF:** Just to see how a non-Transformer model does.
* **ArcheoBERTje Transfer:** Applying the Dutch paper's method directly to see if it works without tweaking.
* **IndicBERT & XLM-R:** To see if other language models were naturally better than mBERT.
* **Our Final Model:** mBERT + CRF + Focal Loss.

We evaluated everything using Precision, Recall, and weighted F1-score. We trained for about 10 epochs using a small learning rate ($2 \times 10^{-5}$) so we wouldn't mess up mBERT's pre-trained weights too much.

## 7. Results
Our final model did noticeably better than the baselines. We achieved a weighted F1 score of 0.7724.

| Model | Precision | Recall | Weighted F1 |
|-------|-----------|--------|-------------|
| BiLSTM-CRF | 0.4850 | 0.4520 | 0.4680 |
| ArcheoBERTje style | 0.5430 | 0.5120 | 0.5270 |
| IndicBERT | 0.5890 | 0.5740 | 0.5814 |
| XLM-RoBERTa | 0.6350 | 0.6120 | 0.6232 |
| **Our Model (mBERT+CRF+FL)** | **0.7121** | **0.8439** | **0.7724** |

![Baselines Comparison](data:image/png;base64,)
*Figure 1: Comparing our final model against the baselines.*

![Baselines Comparison](data:image/png;base64,)
*Figure 1: Comparing our final model against the baselines.*

We were pretty happy with these results. The fact that the ArcheoBERTje style approach only got 0.52 shows that you can't just copy a high-resource method into a low-resource code-mixed problem and expect it to work. 

Also, XLM-R did okay, but we noticed it started overfitting quickly. This might be because it's a huge model and we only had 1,200 sentences, whereas mBERT was just the right size.

When we looked at the individual classes, LOC and PER did really well (around 0.85 F1). This is probably because they are mostly capitalized proper nouns, which makes them easy to spot. On the other hand, MAT and CON struggled a bit more (~0.60 to 0.65 F1).

![Per-Entity Performance](data:image/png;base64,)
*Figure 2: Precision, Recall, and F1 Score for individual entity classes.*

![F1 Curves](data:image/png;base64,)
*Figure 3: Training and Validation F1 Scores over epochs.*

![Per-Entity Performance](data:image/png;base64,)
*Figure 2: Precision, Recall, and F1 Score for individual entity classes.*

![F1 Curves](data:image/png;base64,)
*Figure 3: Training and Validation F1 Scores over epochs.*

### Ablation Study
We did a quick test to see which part of our pipeline helped the most:
* Taking out the Data Augmentation dropped the score by a massive 8.4%. Clearly, just having more data was the biggest factor.
* Taking out Focal Loss dropped the score by about 3.5%. Without it, the model basically forgot the MAT and CON classes existed.
* Taking out the CRF dropped the score by 3%. Without the CRF, the model started predicting broken tag sequences.

## 8. Error Analysis
Even though the model improved, it still makes some funny mistakes.

![Confusion Matrix](data:image/png;base64,)
*Figure 4: Confusion Matrix on our Test Set. Notice the confusion between MAT and ART.*

![Confusion Matrix](data:image/png;base64,)
*Figure 4: Confusion Matrix on our Test Set. Notice the confusion between MAT and ART.*

1. **MAT vs ART Confusion:** The model gets confused between materials and artifacts all the time. For example, if it sees "a bronze seal", it sometimes tags "bronze" as an artifact instead of a material. This happens because structurally, the words look identical to the model.
2. **Boundary Errors:** This happens especially in cases like "red polished ware". The model might correctly grab "red" and "polished" as an artifact, but then it just drops the word "ware". 
3. **Number Issues:** We observed that if there's a measurement next to an object (like "5cm steatite seal"), the model sometimes thinks "5cm" is part of the material name. It seems the context window just lumps them together.

## 9. Code-Mixed Analysis
Hindi words written in English script create a lot of issues. Since words like *Pradakshina* aren't in mBERT's English vocabulary, the tokenizer chops them into 4 or 5 weird subwords. 

We separated out the sentences that were heavily code-mixed from the mostly English ones. We saw that the model's F1 score dropped by about 6% on the heavily mixed sentences. The Transformers really struggle when the syntax jumps between English grammar and Hindi nouns. The CRF layer actually saved us here—even when the BERT embeddings were messy because of the chopping, the CRF knew structurally what tag should come next based on the surrounding English words.

## 10. Discussion
Honestly, we are pretty satisfied with how this turned out. We managed to get a decent NER system working without needing a massive unannotated corpus for pre-training. 

Adding Focal Loss and CRF really helped patch up the weaknesses of not having enough data. However, our biggest limitation right now is the synthetic data augmentation. While it helped the numbers go up, generating sentences using templates might be making the model a bit lazy because it isn't seeing true, wild sentence structures. 

This approach could probably be used for other low-resource code-mixed domains too, like regional legal documents or medical records in India.

## 11. Conclusion
In this project, we tried to build an NER system for Indian archaeological texts. Since we didn't have much data and the text was heavily code-mixed, standard methods didn't work well. By combining mBERT with data augmentation, Focal Loss, and a CRF layer, we managed to push our F1 score up to 0.7724. It’s not perfect, and it still gets confused by materials and artifacts, but it shows that you can build useful tools for niche digital humanities projects if you tweak the architecture the right way. 

For future work, it would be cool to try freezing the model and just using something like LoRA adapters, or maybe actually scraping a few thousand more sentences from the web so we don't have to rely on data augmentation so much.
